1: The toledot of Shem, Ham, and Japheth opens the table of nations — the account of how the whole earth was populated from Noah’s three sons. This table is the map over which Christ’s redemption is drawn: “you purchased for God persons from every tribe and language and people and nation” (Revelation 5:9). Paul at the Areopagus grounds his proclamation of Christ in this table: “From one man he made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth… so that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him” (Acts 17:26–27). The table of nations is the canvas on which the Great Commission is painted.
2: The sons of Japheth — the northern and western peoples (Medes, Greeks, peoples of Asia Minor and the coastlands). These nations are among those present at Pentecost (Acts 2:9–11) when the language-division of Babel begins to be reversed by the Spirit of Christ. The Japhethite nations are part of the scope of the Great Commission: “Go and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:19). The geographic range of Japheth’s descendants marks the reach of the gospel into Europe and beyond.
3: The sons of Gomer — peoples of the far north and northeast. These are nations of the known world’s edge. The gospel’s commission reaches to “the ends of the earth” (Acts 1:8) — precisely to the most distant peoples catalogued here. Even the most remote of the table-of-nations peoples are within the scope of Christ’s “all authority in heaven and on earth” (Matthew 28:18) and his redemptive purpose of gathering “the full number of the Gentiles” (Romans 11:25).
4: The sons of Javan — the Greek peoples, including Tarshish (possibly Spain) and the Kittites (Cyprus and the Mediterranean coastlands). Javan’s descendants include the Greek-speaking world that becomes the vehicle of the NT’s proclamation. The Greek language in which the NT is written, the trade routes of the Mediterranean coastlands, the synagogues of the diaspora — all are the inheritance of the Japhethite-Javanic spread. God’s providential ordering of the nations (Acts 17:26) placed the Greek world exactly where it was needed for the gospel’s spread.
5: “From these the maritime peoples spread out into their territories by their clans within their nations, each with its own language.” The linguistic diversity of the nations — already present here before Babel (or reflecting the post-Babel order) — is the diversity that the Spirit overcomes at Pentecost: “each one heard their own language being spoken” (Acts 2:6). The many languages of the nations, which will become the symbol of division at Babel (Genesis 11), become the medium of proclamation at Pentecost. Christ is declared in every tongue; the linguistic diversity of the nations serves the universal reach of the gospel.
6: The sons of Ham — Egypt, Canaan, Cush, Put. These are the nations that will variously oppress and interact with Israel in the OT narrative. Egypt enslaves Israel; Canaan is the promised land’s occupant; Cush (Ethiopia) features in Psalms 68:31 as a nation that will bring gifts to God. The Ethiopian eunuch in Acts 8 is from Cush — one of the earliest Gentile converts, explicitly fulfilling Isaiah’s vision (Isaiah 56:3–8) and the Psalm’s promise. The Hamitic nations are within the scope of the gospel’s reach from the beginning of Acts.
7: The sons of Cush include Seba, Havilah, Sheba, and Dedan. Sheba and Dedan appear in later prophetic texts as trading nations that will bring tribute to God’s anointed king: “The kings of Sheba and Seba will present him gifts. All kings will bow down to him and all nations will serve him” (Psalms 72:10–11 — a Messianic psalm). The Queen of Sheba comes to Solomon (1 Kings 10:1–13), whom Jesus cites as a type of Gentile seeking wisdom: “The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment… and now something greater than Solomon is here” (Matthew 12:42). The nations catalogued here are the nations Psalm 72 promises will serve Christ.
8: Nimrod is the first named “mighty warrior” (gibbôr) on earth — the prototype of imperial power. The contrast with Christ is instructive: Nimrod is “mighty” through conquest and kingdom-building; Christ is mighty through self-giving and suffering. Philippians 2:6–9 describes the opposite trajectory: not grasping power but emptying, not building a kingdom by force but receiving it through obedience to death. The one who truly receives the kingdoms of the earth (Revelation 11:15 — “the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Messiah”) does so not as a Nimrod but as the Lamb.
9: “A mighty hunter before the LORD” — Nimrod’s fame is established in a proverb. The image of the hunter is picked up in prophetic texts: Jeremiah 16:16 — God sending “hunters” to pursue Israel into exile. Christ as the Good Shepherd (John 10:11) is the counter-image: not the hunter who pursues prey for conquest, but the shepherd who lays down his life for the sheep. The “hunter of men” that Nimrod represents is the template of worldly power; Christ as the one who seeks and saves the lost (Luke 19:10) is the opposite — not hunting to destroy but seeking to save.
10: The first centers of Nimrod’s kingdom: Babylon, Uruk, Akkad, and Kalneh in Shinar. Babylon — the city that begins here — becomes in Scripture the symbol of human empire in opposition to God (Dan 4; Revelation 14:8; 17–18). The “fall of Babylon” in Rev 18 is the decisive defeat of the Nimrod-principle: human civilization organized for self-glorification rather than God’s glory. Christ is the “King of kings and Lord of lords” (Revelation 19:16) before whom all Babylons fall. The origin of Babylon here is the origin of everything that will be judged when Christ returns.
11: From Shinar Nimrod builds Nineveh and the Assyrian cities. Nineveh becomes the target of Jonah’s mission — and Jesus explicitly uses Jonah’s three-day experience as a type of his own death and resurrection (Matthew 12:40). Nineveh’s repentance at Jonah’s preaching is cited by Jesus as a judgment against those who refuse to repent at the proclamation of “something greater than Jonah” (Matthew 12:41). The great Assyrian city founded by Nimrod’s extension of power is also the city that repents — a preview of the Gentile response to the gospel of Christ.
12: Resen between Nineveh and Calah — “the great city.” The description “great city” (hāʿîr haggĕḏōlāh) echoes through Jonah (Genesis 1:2; Genesis 3:2; Genesis 4:11) and enters Revelation as the designation for Babylon (Revelation 11:8; Genesis 16:19; Genesis 18:10). The contrast between the “great city” of human empire (which falls, Revelation 18:10) and the New Jerusalem that comes down from God (Revelation 21:2 — a city “prepared as a bride adorned for her husband”) is the eschatological counter to everything Nimrod’s great cities represent. Christ establishes the only city that lasts.
13: Egypt was the father of the Ludites, Anamites, and the other peoples of northeastern Africa and the Mediterranean. Egypt is the nation into which Abraham descends in the famine (Genesis 12:10), into which Jacob’s family migrates (Genesis 46), from which the Exodus delivers Israel, and into which the holy family flees (Matthew 2:13–15). The origin of Egypt here is the origin of the nation through which the great Exodus-type of redemption will be worked out — the type that Paul applies to Christ: “our ancestors were all under the cloud and… all passed through the sea” and “that rock was Christ” (1 Corinthians 10:1–4).
14: The Kasluhites, from whom the Philistines came, and the Caphtorites. The Philistines — Israel’s persistent OT adversaries — originate in Ham’s line. Goliath the Philistine is the giant-warrior that David defeats — and David’s defeat of Goliath is a type of Christ’s defeat of the adversary. 1 Sam 17’s narrative of the champion who fights on behalf of his people is the pattern that Paul applies to Christ in Romans 5:17–19 and Colossians 2:15. The Philistines who are the enemy of David and his kingdom trace their origin to this verse.
15: Canaan fathered Sidon and the Hittites. Canaan is the promised land — the land God promises to Abraham in the next chapter (Genesis 12:7) and gives to Israel through Joshua. The Canaanite nations listed here are the nations whose land becomes the inheritance of Israel — and the land promise is the type of the inheritance Christ secures: “In him we were also chosen, having been predestined according to the plan of him who works out everything… in order that we might be for the praise of his glory” (Ephesians 1:11–12). The land given to Canaan becomes the land promised to Abraham’s seed — who is Christ (Galatians 3:16).
16: Jebusites, Amorites, Girgashites — among the nations occupying Canaan before Israel. The Jebusites held Jerusalem (Jebus) until David captured it and made it his royal city — the city that becomes the center of the Messianic promise (Psalms 2:6; Genesis 48:1–2; Genesis 132:13–14) and the city where Christ is crucified, raised, and from which the gospel goes out (Acts 1:8). The Jebusites’ stronghold that David took becomes the city that Christ enters on Palm Sunday, dies outside its walls, and transforms into the type of the heavenly Jerusalem (Hebrews 12:22; Revelation 21:2).
17: Hivites, Arkites, Sinites — further Canaanite clans. These are among the seven nations that God will dispossess to give Israel the land (Deuteronomy 7:1). The dispossession of the Canaanites is not ethnic cleansing but divine judgment on entrenched wickedness (Genesis 15:16 — “the sin of the Amorites has not yet reached its full measure”). The same divine justice that expels the Canaanites will, in Christ, offer them (and all nations) mercy through repentance (Rahab in Josh 2; the Canaanite woman in Matthew 15:21–28). Christ’s redemption reaches even those nations that stand under judgment.
18: Arvadites, Zemarites, Hamathites — the northernmost Canaanite clans, with Hamath marking the ideal northern border of the promised land (Numbers 34:8; 1 Kings 8:65). The full extent of the Canaanite territory — from Sidon in the north to the cities of the plain in the south — is the extent of the inheritance that God promises and that Christ the seed of Abraham ultimately inherits (Galatians 3:16, 29). The Canaanite clans scattered across this territory are the peoples over whose land the promise of the seed will be fulfilled.
19: The borders of Canaan reaching toward Sodom and Gomorrah. Sodom and Gomorrah — which will be destroyed in Genesis 19 — become in the NT the paradigm of divine judgment against sin. Jesus uses Sodom explicitly as a warning to cities that reject the gospel: “It will be more bearable for Sodom on the day of judgment than for you” (Matthew 10:15; Genesis 11:24). The geography of the Canaanite border that includes Sodom and Gomorrah is the geography of the judgment that Christ’s coming makes more severe for those who reject it than it was for those ancient cities.
20: The summary of the Hamitic nations — Ham’s clans by their languages, territories, and nations. The closing formula emphasizes the diversity of human civilization: clans, languages, territories, nations. This fourfold diversity is exactly the fourfold description of Christ’s redeemed in Revelation 5:9 (“from every tribe and language and people and nation”) and Revelation 7:9 (“a great multitude that no one could count, from every nation, tribe, people and language”). The table of nations ends each section with this formula, as if already anticipating the full harvest that Christ will gather from each category.
21: “Sons were also born to Shem… Shem was the ancestor of all the sons of Eber.” Shem’s line is the Semitic line — the line from which Abraham, Israel, and Christ descend. The emphasis that Shem is “the ancestor of all the sons of Eber” — the Hebrews (‘ibrîm from Eber) — marks this line as the line through which God’s redemptive purpose will narrow from all humanity to one nation and ultimately to one person. The Semitic line is the line Christ enters: “a descendant of David according to the flesh” (Romans 1:3), a Hebrew of Hebrews (Philippians 3:5).
22: The sons of Shem include Arphaxad and Aram. Arphaxad is the direct ancestor of Abraham (Genesis 11:10–26) and thus of Christ. Luke 3:36 names Arphaxad in the genealogy of Jesus between Shem and Shelah. The Arameans (from Aram) become Israel’s neighbors and sometimes enemies; Israel’s confession “My father was a wandering Aramean” (Deuteronomy 26:5) identifies Aramaic origins. The language of Jesus’ daily speech was Aramaic — a descendant of Aram’s influence. The Son of God speaks in the tongue of Aram’s descendants.
23: The sons of Aram include Uz. The land of Uz is where Job lives (Job 1:1) — the setting of one of the OT’s most profound explorations of suffering and divine justice. Job’s anguish and his cry for a mediator (Job 9:33 — “if only there were someone to mediate between us”) is the cry that Christ answers as the one mediator between God and humanity (1 Timothy 2:5). The Aram-Uz connection threads from this genealogical entry to the book of Job to the NT’s answer to Job’s desperate hope for a mediator.
24: Arphaxad fathers Shelah; Shelah fathers Eber. Both are named in Luke’s genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:35). The name Eber gives the Hebrews their name (ʿibrî) — the people descended from the line that leads to Abraham and ultimately to Christ. The Hebrew people — the “people of Eber” — are the people through whom the promise of Genesis 3:15 is carried and through whom Christ enters the world. This genealogical chain is the chain of promise.
25: Peleg — “because in his time the earth was divided.” The division of the earth likely refers to the Babel event of Genesis 11. Luke 3:35 names Peleg in Christ’s genealogy. The generation of the earth’s division is represented in Christ’s ancestry — Christ comes not despite the division of humanity into nations but through it, and in order to gather what was divided: “I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen. I must bring them also” (John 10:16). The one born of Peleg’s divided-earth generation will re-unite what was scattered at Babel through the gospel.
26: Joktan’s line — the Arabian peoples. Like the other branches of the table of nations, these peoples are within the scope of Christ’s redemptive reach. The gospel moves into Arabia: Paul went to Arabia after his conversion (Galatians 1:17), and the Arabian peoples are implied in the global scope of Acts 1:8 (“to the ends of the earth”) and Revelation 7:9 (“every nation, tribe, people and language”). No branch of the table of nations is outside the scope of the Great Commission.
27: The sons of Joktan continue — Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah. These are Arabian tribal names. They represent the diversity of the human family that Christ is sent to redeem. The scope of John 3:16 — “God so loved the world” — is measured by the breadth of the table of nations. Every clan listed in Genesis 10 is within the “world” that God loves and that Christ comes to save. The table of nations is the table of the lost who become the found.
28: Obal, Abimael, Sheba — further Arabian peoples. Sheba recurs in the prophetic literature of the OT as a nation that will honor Israel’s king: “May the kings of Sheba and Seba present him gifts; all kings will bow to him, all nations will serve him” (Psalms 72:10–11). The Queen of Sheba who comes to Solomon is cited by Jesus as a Gentile who sought wisdom and who will testify at the judgment against those who reject “something greater than Solomon” (Matthew 12:42). Sheba’s repeated appearance in Messianic prophecy begins with this genealogical entry.
29: Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab — completing Joktan’s line. Ophir is famous as the source of Solomon’s gold (1 Kings 9:28) — the wealth of the nations flowing to Jerusalem as a preview of Isaiah 60:5 and Revelation 21:24. Havilah appeared first in Genesis 2:11 as the gold-rich land of Eden. The echo of Eden-gold reappearing in this genealogy suggests the creational abundance that is being redistributed across the nations — and that will finally be gathered into the New Jerusalem when Christ reigns (Revelation 21:26 — “the glory and honor of the nations will be brought into it”).
30: The geographic range of Joktan’s descendants — from Mesha to Sephar in the eastern hill country. The geographical anchoring of these ancient peoples in real-world space underlines that redemptive history unfolds in the real world. Christ enters the real world — “born in Bethlehem of Judea in the days of Herod the king” (Matthew 2:1) — not a mythological location but a mappable place in the real geography that the table of nations describes. The same God who placed these peoples in their territories sent his Son into a specific territory at a specific time (Galatians 4:4).
31: The closing formula for Shem’s line: “by their clans and languages, in their territories and nations.” The fourfold classification — clans, languages, territories, nations — is the exact fourfold description of the redeemed in Revelation 5:9 and Genesis 7:9. The Spirit who inspired Moses to describe the nations in this fourfold way also inspired John to describe Christ’s redeemed in the same fourfold way — deliberately showing that the nations catalogued here are the nations gathered under Christ.
32: “From these the nations spread out over the earth after the flood.” The summary of the table of nations — all humanity from Noah’s three sons. Paul at Athens quotes this moment of divine ordering: “From one man he made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and he marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands. God did this so that they would seek him and perhaps reach out for him and find him, though he is not far from any one of us” (Acts 17:26–27). The purpose of the spreading of the nations — divinely ordered even in the post-flood world — is that they might seek and find God, who sends his Son to be found (John 1:11–12).
